abstract: This study explores the relationship between psychological maturity for marriage and emotional intelligence among young men. In the context of this research, marital maturity is conceptualized as a multi-dimensional construct comprising three core pillars: self-acceptance, internal integration (encompassing responsibility and a realistic worldview), and a constructive approach to marriage and family life. Utilizing data from a sample of 90 men aged 21–26, the study compared participants with high and low levels of marital readiness. The findings indicate that men with higher levels of psychological maturity for marriage demonstrate significantly greater emotional intelligence than their less mature peers. This link is primarily evidenced by a superior ability to identify, understand, and leverage emotional states to effectively navigate life challenges and interpersonal conflicts, suggesting that emotional competence is a vital foundation for building stable, long-term relationships.
